[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Studies on Joseph Conrad—Iv. the Flow and Ebb of Artistry

Bernard Meyer M.D.

Bernard Meyer

Bernard C. Meyer, M.D.
240 East 62 Street
New York, N. Y. 10021

Bernard Meyer  

IN CLOSING his perceptive and thoughtful study of Conrad, Moser (25) observes: "It is indeed difficult to conquer the impulse to find greatness in all the works of a man we sense to be great." Yet in the interest of discriminating criticism it is surely an impulse which should be resisted: not all of Beethoven is sublime and some of Shakespeare is quite dull. Indeed, it requires no great knowledge of the world or its inhabitants to come to the conclusion that even the Supreme Creator had his off moments.

A comparable unevenness in the quality of Conrad's literary art has been apparent to discerning readers almost from the very inception of his career. Nor was Conrad himself unaware of the fluctuations of his own artistry, for he made no secret of his diverse opinions of his work. It should be emphasized, however, that the artist is not always the best judge of his art, nor can public acclaim be taken as a gauge of quality, for popularity and success are sometimes generated by considerations far removed from the recognition of aesthetic merit. Indeed, Conrad's arrival at the frontier of fame followed the publication in 1913 of Chance(5b), a book which for many critics is an inferior work, and one which ushered in a phase of relatively unrelieved mediocrity that continued and increased until his death in 1924. Such an opinion was held by his friend Galsworthy (10), who wrote, " Chance

Attending Psychiatrist, The Mount Sinai Hospital, New York.

The present paper is the last in a series of four essays. Part I, The Family Romance; Part II, Fetishism; and Part III, Aspects of Orality, have appeared in previous issues of this Journal.

Submitted May 6, 1963

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Comments on the Pleasure-Unpleasure Experience—The Role of Biological Factors

William Needles M.D.

William Needles

William Needles, M.D.
45 East 82 Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

William Needles  

SUMMARY

1. Five stages in the development of Freud's efforts to supply a metapsychological explanation of the pleasure-unpleasure experience are traced, the basis for each successive shift in formulation indicated, and Freud's dissatisfaction with the end result of his labors noted.

2. Attempts of subsequent investigators to amplify and refine the theory are delineated. Certain modifications were seen to derive from more precise phenomenological analysis, the application of more recent concepts of ego psychology, and the replacement of the constancy principle in psychic functioning by the homeostatic principle.

3. Evidence is presented to support the argument that metapsychological considerations alone are insufficient to account for the pleasure-unpleasure experience and that biological and evolutionary factors—in other words, our biological heritage—must be included in any such attempt.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Repetition Compulsion

Sanford Gifford M.D.

Sanford Gifford

Sanford Gifford  

Arthur F. Valenstein opened the panel by defining the two aspects, clinical and theoretical, that had prompted Freud to develop his concept of the repetition compulsion. He described Freud's concern with certain clinical phenomena: the fact that apparently well-analyzed patients failed to lose their symptoms or developed equivalent ones; the problem of destiny neurosis; and the occurrence of repetitions in analysis, which had suggested to Freud the existence of an innate, instinctual tendency to repeat unpleasant experiences that could not be explained by the pleasure principle. Although this phenomenon led Freud to a major revision of instinct theory, to a dualistic concept of aggressive and sexual drives, of primary destructiveness invested in the self and of primary masochism comparable to primary narcissism, the concept of the repetition compulsion, according to Valenstein, remained a clinical construct and became an important part of clinical psychoanalysis.

At the same time, "in keeping with a philosophic predilection … for ultimate questions, " Freud used the repetition compulsion as the basis for a more speculative, abstract, general theory of the life and death instincts, "a superordinal theory of the second order, " including the dual instinct theory but extending metapsychology into the realms of biology and metaphysics, beyond the theoretical requirements of clinical psychoanalysis. Thus the theory of the life and death instincts is a biological rather than a psychological one, but, Valenstein proposed, there is no true contradiction. Psychoanalysis itself is a psychology derived from biology and, as it becomes increasingly a developmental psychology concerned with the earliest phases of life, biological considerations are increasingly important.

Valenstein concluded with a plea that we maintain a clear distinction between clinical and theoretical aspects of the repetition compulsion.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Evidence in Psychoanalytic Research

Benjamin Wolman Ph.D.

Benjamin Wolman

Benjamin B. Wolman, Ph.D.
12 West 96 Street
New York 25, N.Y.

Benjamin Wolman  

SUMMARY

Five types of psychoanalytic propositions have been distinguished. The first three describe empirical data, namely, (1) observable patterns of overt behavior; (2) introspectively observable patterns of consciousness; (3) unobservable, inferable unconscious processes. A variety of research methods is applicable to prove or disprove these propositions, provided these methods develop a sufficiently sophisticated research design and measure what they intend to measure.

No direct empirical evidence is possible in regard to the fourth category of propositions, i.e., to psychoanalytic theory. A theory is tested by its inner consistency, agreement with empirical data, and usefulness. Theory is not a statement of fact and can be modified as research progresses.

The fifth category of propositions describes psychoanalysis as a treatment method. These propositions require new research techniques.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Aspects of that Type of Communication Termed "Small Talk"

Peter Martin M.D.

Peter Martin

Peter A. Martin, M.D.
857 Fisher Bldg.
Detroit 2, Mich.

Peter Martin  

SUMMARY

During their analyses, several patients complained of a difficulty in carrying on an everyday type of communication involving the seemingly unimportant daily subjects of conversation that encompass "small talk." This difficulty varied from mild inhibition to severe symptom formation. The material from one of these analyses is presented.

The material investigated led to the conclusion that the problem is primarily the result of disturbed object relations. The affected individual reacts to a current social situation with an unresolved transference reaction involving fusion or linkage fantasies. It is suggested that the normal functions of the ego facilitating verbal communication are not operative from this fixation point. Separation and individuation are prerequisites for normal development of the ego function of speech.

responseSet.title: The Role of Prediction in Theory Building in Psychoanalysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role of Prediction in Theory Building in Psychoanalysis

Robert Wallerstein M.D.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein, M.D.
The Menninger Foundation
Topeka, Kansas

Robert Wallerstein  

SUMMARY

I have discussed the role of prediction in relation to theory building in psychoanalysis, with special regard to the theory of psychoanalytic therapy. Though prediction as both a principle and an operational tool plays a central role in the growth and development of most areas of science, its place in our science is much less firmly secured. Freud and many analysts after him have been skeptical of psychoanalysis ever being more than a postdictive science. This position is based largely on the multiplicity of determinants that converge on human behavior as retrospectively unraveled in psychoanalysis, and the wide variety of possible evolutions of human behavior as viewed prospectively from any vantage point in time.

Despite this, as a principle, the predictive process, though usually not made explicit, is basic to all clinical analytic work; every responsible action in analytic diagnosis and treatment actually involves predictions, usually unacknowledged and only

690

implicit, derived empirically from the clinical data of the consulting room and/or deductively from the propositions of psychoanalytic theory. However, the effort to render these predictive implications explicit is beset by severe difficulties. A major one is that whenever one predicts future behaviors, one implies a concomitant prediction of an uncontrollable and often unspecifiable future environment, within which and in response to which those behaviors will occur. Some predictive efforts in psychoanalysis have sought to bypass this problem of the ever-changing environment by collapsing the time dimension and limiting themselves to short-term predictions.

Within the Psychotherapy Research Project of The Menninger Foundation, an effort is made at explicit long-term prediction of the course and outcome of psychoanalytic treatment, derived from the comprehensive assessment of the nature of the patient's personality structure and illness and from the postulates of the psychoanalytic theory of therapy. This is done despite the uncertainties in regard to the specific conditions of the future, on the basis of a conception of the "average expectable psychoanalysis" and concomitantly of the "average expectable environment" in which likewise relative stability and accountability is assumed. And additionally, one can predict to the consequences of crucial environmental contingencies that have some knowable likelihood of occurrence. All of this is set up within a tripartite "if-then-because" logical prediction model, in the form " if these conditions are fulfilled, then these consequences will eventuate, because of these assumptions grounded in our theory of psychoanalytic therapy."

It is this last because clause that enables us to use the confirmation or refutation of predictions to strengthen or weaken the credibility of the various theoretical propositions that together make up the fabric of the psychoanalytic theory of therapy. In this sense, via the method of predictions explicated in this fashion, the data of psychoanalysis can be systematically and more precisely linked to the theory of psychoanalysis, and prediction used as a wedge by which to test theory.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Applicants Rejected for Psychoanalytic Training

Henry Fox M.D. , Edward Daniels M.D. and Henry Wermer M.D.

Henry Wermer

Henry M. Fox, M.D.
Edward M. Daniels, M.D.
Henry Wermer, M.D.
721 Huntington Avenue
Boston 15, Mass.

Henry Wermer  

SUMMARY

1. One hundred recent decisions of an admissions committee have been reviewed as a contribution to the discussion of selection for psychoanalytic training.

2. Consistent qualitative as well as quantitative differences were reported in the personality structure of those rejected as compared with the group accepted outright for training.

3. The overlapping categories of characteristics with the inclusion of primitive defense patterns such as projection and denial reported by those who interviewed the group of rejected applicants form a striking contrast to the absence of pregenitally rooted defenses associated with some of the same characteristics among those accepted.

4. The individual voting patterns of the interviewers did not have any clearly demonstrable effect on the final decision.

5. The cultural background and the amount of previous therapy including psychoanalysis did not distinguish the group rejected from those accepted for training.

6. Although many of the rejected applicants were considered good candidates for therapeutic analysis, review of the reasons for rejecting more than half of them for psychoanalytic training helps to clarify opinions concerning the limitations of a training analysis as a procedure for the effective modification of certain ego patterns.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Theory in Relation to the Nosology of Childhood Psychic Disorders

Calvin Settlage M.D.

Calvin Settlage

Calvin F. Settlage, M.D.
St. Christopher's Hospital for Children
2603 North Fifth Street
Philadelphia 33, Pa.

Calvin Settlage  

SUMMARY

This paper aims at deriving the framework of a workable and meaningful nosological system for childhood psychic disorders. Psychogenesis is presented in terms of the timing of traumatic experience and the resulting fixation points in relation to the development of object relationship, the phases of early ego development, and the stages of psychosexual development. The attainment of object constancy is seen to be a critical factor in determining whether traumatic experience will eventuate in a neurotic or psychotic type of disorder.

The concepts of neurotic and of psychotic character disorders are discussed and illustrated, and it is suggested that these disorders

799

can be differentiated respectively from neuroses and psychoses on the basis of the degree of impairment in object relationship, the extent of ego impairment, and the amount of distortion of perception of internal and external reality, as it results from the ego defensive and adaptive techniques employed in dealing with internal and external dangers.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narcissism and the Ego Ideal

John Murray M.D.

John Murray

John M. Murray, M.D.
82 Marlborough Street
Boston 16, Mass.

John Murray  

SUMMARY

Freud's earliest investigations revealed to him the importance of infantile sexuality in the etiology of the neuroses. This led to his first formulations of the theoretical aspects of the instinctual and libidinal life of man and his theories of neuroses. Then came the Three Contributions to the Theory of Sexuality, to be followed by the classical case history papers and the Schreber case. Next, he turned to the papers on the transformation of instincts and allied phenomena, which in turn led him to the papers on the ego. These magnificent contributions deal largely with the instinctual and libidinal. Having built a solid foundation for the theoretical aspects of these factors, he went on to erect the superstructure of ego psychology, again largely from the libidinal point of view.

508

In his paper, "On Narcissism: An Introduction, " he again built a solid foundation for the theoretical formulation of the problem of narcissism, in relation to ego structure, as he had done earlier with the instincts. However, this was never followed up with special papers devoted to the vicissitudes and destiny of narcissism in health and in neurosis, as he did with his thoughts on sublimation and transformation of instincts. In speaking of the high goals of analytic treatment, Freud (14) said: "We seek rather to enrich him [the patient] from his own internal sources, by putting at the disposal of his ego those energies which, owing to repression, are inaccessibly confined in his unconscious, as well as those which his ego is obliged to squander in the fruitless task of maintaining these repressions. Such work as this is spiritual guidance in the best sense of the words. … It is only by carrying on our analytic spiritual guidance that we can deepen our dawning comprehension of the human mind." In our therapeutic endeavors we cannot fulfill the essential elements Freud embraced in his conception of "carrying on our spiritual guidance" without a deep and clearly defined understanding of the ego, and most particularly in the area of the ego ideal. The poet, Robert Frost, has said, "Freedom is moving easy in harness." He who is non-neurotic has found this freedom and lives in a healthy balance of the expression of instinctual demands and legitimate restraints. The mature ego ideal is a most important force in maintaining the integrity and permanence of this balance, so essential to emotional health.

This paper has tried to contribute clinical material and preliminary formulations in order to continue developments from Freud's basic thoughts in his paper, "On Narcissism, " and to provide working conceptions of the ego ideal. Our literature needs further elaboration in theoretical understanding, based upon writings from clinical experience which will parallel the historical development in the libidinal and instinctual areas. Clinical experience forms the best base for deeper theoretical considerations of narcissism and its role in ego development, symptom formation, and defense.

The present paper deals with several pertinent considerations:

509

1. "The ego ideal is the heir of the original narcissism" (10).

2. The ego ideal is transformed from narcissism by a change of aim and object relationship akin to sublimation of instincts; i.e., an abstraction may replace a narcissistic hunger.

3. The ego ideal may lose its integrity and regress to the level of its earlier narcissistic origins.

4. The ego ideal tends to function antithetically to the narcissistic entitlements of the pregenital era. The failure of its proper development is evident in severe neuroses by persistence of pregenital characteristics.

5. The ego ideal may exist, in schizoid personalities, in a fragmented form, split off from residues of primitive narcissistic functioning, and these two elements may alternate in dominating ego activities.

6. The ego ideal may undergo extensive growth and transformation from narcissism in neurotic or psychotic conditions as a result of psychoanalytic modification.

7. The ego ideal has libidinal objects as part of its organization, and the affects may undergo sublimation.

[[RunningHead]]

Exhibitionism and Fascination

Alfred Siegman M.D.

Alfred Siegman

Alfred J. Siegman, M.D.
170 East 78 Street
New York, N.Y. 10021

Alfred Siegman  

SUMMARY

I have attempted to demonstrate that exhibitionism is associated with the striving to master states of infantile fascination that were passively experienced. The phenomenon of fascination was studied and correlated with early ego states when self and object are incompletely differentiated and both pleasurable and unpleasurable sensations are perceived as being induced by an omnipotent object. It was hypothesized that the form and quality of the exhibitionism, along with the concomitant fears and ability to sublimate, are determined by the phase-specific coloration of significant fascinating experiences and the ratio of aggressive and libidinal elements. Clinical examples were offered to illustrate the overdetermination of the exhibitionistic act. Finally, although exhibitionism is usually associated with visual impressions and the urge to see (scoptophilia), this is not always the case, indicating that exhibitionism and scoptophilia are not complete counterparts.

[[RunningHead]]

The Wolf Man Grows Older

Muriel Gardiner M.D.

Muriel Gardiner

Muriel M. Gardiner, M.D.
Brookdale Farm
Pennington, N.J.

Muriel Gardiner  

THE WOLF MAN is one of the first and oldest of all psychoanalytic patients now living, and the one about whose life we psychoanalysts know most. Therefore it seems that what we can learn about his attitude toward growing older can contribute something to psychoanalytic considerations of old age. In the thirty-five years I have known the Wolf Man, he has always seemed to me old, and yet in another way he has always seemed young. The tragedies of his life, his frequent depressions, and his pessimism have made him seem older than his years. But his appearance has been, and is, that of a younger man, and so is his conversation when he is animated and interested. Now, at seventy-six, his sensory perceptions and mental powers are intact. My husband, who saw the Wolf Man in Vienna in the summer of 1962, described him to friends as "so young, so intelligent and interesting. And a handsome man."

In March, 1963, I wrote to the Wolf Man asking him certain questions about his attitude toward growing older, and requesting his permission to publish this material. I shall quote his long answer word for word. As was to be foreseen, his answers to my questions do not tell us as much as the spontaneous remarks I invited him to make.

This paper, substantially as it appears here, was presented as part of the Panel on Psychoanalytic Considerations of Old Age at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, St. Louis, May, 1963.

Submitted June 21, 1963

[[RunningHead]]

Early Development and Endowment of the Artistic Director

Philip Weissman M.D.

Philip Weissman

Philip Weissman, M.D.
1148 Fifth Ave.
New York 28, N.Y.

Philip Weissman  

SUMMARY

Specific aspects of the artistic director's early development during and after the oedipal period exert dynamic effects upon the shaping and the functioning of his career. The unconscious oedipal

77

wish to rear the parents' children finds expression and gratification in his directorial activities. The wish to rear the parents' children may come from either a positive or negative oedipal configuration from which then ensues an identification with the parent of the same or opposite sex respectively. Regardless of its negative or positive oedipal origin, the identification is utilized via a sublimated wish to direct the performers as children. A common parental combination for the artistic director is one in which the mother has strong aesthetic interests, usually in the performing area, and the father is involved in executive, managerial, or directorial activities. One artistic director and two nonartistic directors had mothers who had no aesthetic interests; however, all three fathers fulfilled the requirements of managerial or executive positions. The artistic director often identifies with the ego ideals of both parents which may encompass the father's love and support for the mother's aesthetic interests.

The artistic director's unconscious oedipal wish to rear his parents' children facilitates his identification with the creator whose work he treats and presents as if it were his own child. The dynamics of the psychological interplay among the creator, director, and performer have been noted. Summed up, the performer is the child in whom the parentally identified director has instilled the re-created essence of the work of the composer or the dramatist—the true parent.

[[RunningHead]]

Some Considerations Regarding the Ego Ideal in the Psychoanalytic Process

Grete Bibring M.D.

Grete Bibring

Grete L. Bibring, M.D.
47 Garden Street
Cambridge 38, Mass.

Grete Bibring  

SUMMARY

The successful analysis is carried by the transference with its revival of infantile impulses and wishes. Transference provides the basis from which we start, makes possible the modification and temporary changes of the superego, and leads to the therapeutic alliance. The ego is set free for its effort to understand and to disentangle itself from blind pressures from both id and superego. The final achievement of this process lies in insight into and understanding of infantile conflicts, defenses, and guilt. At the same time new identifications with a new and mature superego and ego ideal take place which go pari passu with the increasing ability and necessity of achieving sublimation. There are patients whose serious neurotic disturbance is due to an early developmental arrest or severe traumata, initiating a defensive regressive move into the realm of infantile narcissistic aggrandizement. In his paper "Narcissism and the Ego Ideal" Murray demonstrated convincingly for a series of such patients the importance of the reorganization of the ego ideal. Indispensable and difficult as it may be for these patients with a deeply rooted narcissistic ego ideal to bring about the necessary changes, this same process represents, in a less arduous form, a factor of importance in every analysis which we bring to a successful conclusion.

Psychoanalysis has convincingly brought home to psychologists, educators, and sociologists the fact that the vicissitudes of early life have far-reaching consequences for later development. Every new contribution Freud made to the understanding of the neuroses was eagerly seized upon by pedagogues and psychologists. Thus, in the earliest stages of analysis, it was thought that Victorian prudishness had resulted in repression of childhood sexuality, and it was hoped that neurosis could be avoided if children were given greater freedom in this area.

Soon afterward it was discovered that the oedipus complex exerts far-reaching influences on the development of personality and that it seemed to be at the root of most neurotic problems. Parents then strove to be aloof and nongiving to their children, in the expectation that the arousal of oedipal feelings could be avoided. Unfortunately, this only resulted in a blunting of emotional expression.

When analysts discovered the importance of the aggressive drives, it was held that the free expression of aggression and aggressive acts would be important

[[RunningHead]]

Phyllis Greenacre—A Tribute

Heinz Kohut M.D.

Heinz Kohut

Heinz Kohut  

It is my privilege to say a few words about Dr. Phyllis Greenacre, the speaker of this plenary session, and to give expression to the esteem and the affection which psychoanalysts feel toward her.

Why is Dr. Greenacre important to us? Why have we honored her in the past and are honoring her today? The answer seems easy: it is, of course, predominantly out of respect for her as a creative contributor to our science, out of gratitude for her work. And surely, it is not difficult to justify our admiration by even the briefest review of her writings.

She has indeed given us a great deal. There are her papers on clinical topics, containing what may well be the outstanding examples of the use of case material in modern psychoanalytic literature; technical papers concerning specific problems posed by the clinical transference; more theoretically oriented contributions to our understanding of personality functioning and of the influence of early environment; and characterological studies, distinguished by a judicious appreciation of the inextricable amalgamation of deficits and assets in the structure of personality. Then there are the promising and original observations and reflections on the nature of creativity and on some specific determinants of the creative personality (such as the essay on "The Childhood of the Artist, " which, I believe, Dr. Greenacre herself would consider among the favorite products of her own creativeness); and there are the enchanting treatises on the ordinary weaknesses of extraordinary men (the studies on Swift and Carroll, for example) in

Editor's Note: It is a most fortunate coincidence that the publication of Dr. Greenacre's address given before the plenary session of the American Psychoanalytic Association last fall also offers an opportunity to pay tribute to her on her seventieth birthday on May 3, 1964. This we felt could be done most appropriately by publishing Dr. Heinz Kohut's introductory remarks on the occasion of the presentation of this paper.

Freud and Jones

William Niederland M.D.

William Niederland

William G. Niederland, M.D.
1143 Fifth Avenue
New York 28, N.Y.

William Niederland  

I

The writing of biographies, one of the oldest literary practices, holds an impressive and, on the whole, honorable position in the history of intellectual pursuits. From K. R. Eissler's recent study on Leonardo da Vinci(5) and Thomas Mann's Leiden und Grösse der Meister(14) to Plutarch's Lives and to some extent still further back (to the ancient chronicles in the Book of Judges, the Book of Kings, and in poetic or legendary form perhaps all the way to the Gilgamesh epic) runs the long line of intellectual endeavors to re-create the essence of a human life in verbal shape and, if possible, aesthetic fashion.

Biography, under the influence of psychoanalytic insight, has long ceased to be a summation of factual or fancied accounts. In its reconstructive aspects it has been correctly likened to the analyst's effort to recover, unravel, and connect significant data aimed at the psychological reconstruction of a given personage in his relation to individual evolvement, origins, surroundings, achievements, and ultimate fate. In a sense every individual psychoanalysis is a biographical effort, though not limited to it, of course, in its reparative and therapeutic scope. The kinship between analytic interpretations in daily practice and biographical ones in modern literature has often been mentioned. However, relevant dissimilarities should also be noted, aside from the truism that in one case living data are derived from a living person ("a dead 'patient' does not talk back"), while in the case of biographical work inquiry ordinarily deals with a more silent, though not necessarily less eloquent

Bernard Meyer M.D.

Bernard Meyer

Bernard C. Meyer, M.D.
240 East 62 Street

Bernard Meyer  

ON THE EVE of Conrad's departure for his triumphal visit to America in April, 1923, his wife Jessie learned to her dismay that their elder son Borys, then twenty-five years old, had secretly married (6). Endowed with an unusually sensitive understanding of her somewhat eccentric husband, and aware of the strained nature of the relationship between father and son, Jessie feared that disclosing the news might well ruin her husband's impending trip, if it did not prompt him to cancel it altogether. With a characteristic display of courage, therefore, she elected to keep the secret to herself, and Conrad sailed for New York untroubled by personal domestic concerns. Although stage fright probably caused him to restrict his public appearances to a minimum, his brief tour was a decided success and signified that he was now recognized on both sides of the Atlantic as an acknowledged master of English prose. The trip was a success in another respect too, for it was accompanied by a subjective state of relative good health, itself an unusual condition for Conrad during the latter half of his life.

Immediately upon his return home in June, however, the long-delayed bombshell exploded when, after they had retired to bed for the night, Jessie broke the news about Borys.

IN DECEMBER, 1920, a thirty-three-year-old psychiatrist by the name of Girindrasekhar Bose addressed the following letter to Freud:

Dear Sir:

I take the liberty to send you under separate cover a book entitled Concept of Repression as an insignificant presentation from its author.

Along with my friends and relations I have been a warm admirer of your theories and science; and it might interest you to learn that your name has been a household word in our family for the past decade.

I shall be delighted to have your opinion and suggestions about my work. Hoping to hear from you in due course,

I remain, Dear Sir,
Yours very sincerely
G.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Reports—Prepuberty and Child Analysis

Eleanor Galenson M.D.

Eleanor Galenson

Eleanor Galenson  

Eveoleen N. Rexford described the purpose of the meeting as a free exploration of our concept of the prepuberty phase, a definition of its characteristics, and a consideration of the usefulness of such a concept for theoretical and clinical activities today. A brief case presentation of an analysis of a ten-year-old boy by Helen R. Beiser was to be used as the basis for the discussion.

Throughout the presentations which followed, the phase of prepuberty was discussed from four major viewpoints: the anatomical, physiological, endocrinological, and psychological. However, there was a consistent emphasis upon the correlation of these aspects, and any neat division would do an injustice to this attempt at integration.

In the opening brief communications, Anne Benjamin referred to Helene Deutsch's characterization of prepuberty in girls as "a last stage of latency, " with a thrust toward reality adaptation. This differs from the point of view put forth by Peter Blos in his publications. He considers prepuberty in boys as the earliest phase of adolescence with a quantitative increase of instinctual drive manifestations and a revival of all earlier modes of gratification. Margaret Fries supports Blos's view of prepuberty as the earliest phase of adolescence when she speaks of ego integration and consolidation before the onset of increased sexual drives of prepuberty. Benjamin defined prepuberty in physiological terms, beginning with the first appearance of the secondary sexual changes and ending with the menarche in girls and ejaculation in boys. She considered the growth spurt another physiological change which has a definite effect upon the body image, and observed that concomitant psychological characteristics for this age period might be expected.

Presented at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December, 1963. Chairman: Eveoleen N. Rexford, M.D.

Bernard Meyer M.D.

Bernard Meyer

Bernard C. Meyer, M.D.
240 East 62 Street
New York, N.Y. 10021

Bernard Meyer  

THE RECOGNITION that the origins of those aspects of human behavior, mental functioning, and affective mood which are heavily stamped with the imprint of orality lie within the earliest years of childhood, and arise as a psychic distillate of the initial interaction between mother and child, causes the present study to labor under something of a disadvantage, for pertinent data concerning intimate features of Conrad's early life are extremely sparse. Some few facts are known, to be sure, and there are hints, recorded elsewhere (21b), of the nature of the character of Conrad's mother, Evelina Korzeniowska. But for the ears of clinicians, accustomed to hearing the insistent and reiterated strains of the songs of childhood of their patients, these data and these hints must seem faint indeed. In the present study I am obliged, therefore, to focus attention upon comments supplied by observers of Conrad as a grown man, and to combine these observations with the self-revelatory utterances voiced in his literary art. Fortunately, the abundance and richness of both these sources are sufficiently great and their meanings sufficiently transparent to compensate for the relative inadequacy of his early history.

Some Aspects of Pathological Narcissism

Thomas Freeman M.D.

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman, M.D.
Lansdowne Clinic
4 Royal Cresent
Glasgow, C.3
Scotland

Thomas Freeman  

SUMMARY

An attempt has been made in this paper to utilize the concept of narcissism to demonstrate the psychopathological processes envisaged as occurring in different mental states. The concept of pathological narcissism is defined, and the forms it may assume in neurosis, sexual perversion, and psychosis are described with the aid of clinical data. The concept of pathological narcissism may be useful in examining the relationship between neurosis and psychosis.

Some Clinical Considerations of the Ego Ideal

Helene Deutsch M.D.

Helene Deutsch

Helene Deutsch, M.D.
44 Larchwood Drive
Cambridge 38, Mass.

Helene Deutsch  

SINCE FREUD'S publication of the paper on narcissism, analytic contributions to the subject of the ego ideal have to a great extent been a continuation of speculations set forth in that paper (5). The theoretical approach to the ego ideal gives us above all the history of its development. This is an oscillation between projection and introjection, identifications with parents (or their successors), the interplay between ego ideal and superego. The end result is an ideal on a high level of cultural values.

On the other hand, the clinical approach forces us to dilute the conception of "cultural demands" into levels more in agreement with the vast majority of the population. Looking at the cultural background from the more individual standpoint, as an influence of personal milieu, we can understand better the differences in ego ideals. The variety of personalities is not only determined by instinct development and defense mechanisms but also by the formation of the ego ideal.

In some individuals the ego ideal is more abstract, directed inward. In others it has to be externalized and gratified by action. At one end we find the ascetic saint, on the other an individual whose ego ideal is under the rule of the pleasure principle, and even achieved by directly sexual means.

An excellent example is the case cited by Abraham (1): A young man (probably one of his patients)

responseSet.title: The Derailment of Dialogue—Stimulus Overload, Action Cycles, and the Completion Gradient
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Derailment of Dialogue—Stimulus Overload, Action Cycles, and the Completion Gradient

René Spitz M.D.

René Spitz

René A. Spitz, M.D.
12 Rue Robert de Traz
Geneva, Switzertand

René Spitz  

SUMMARY

Systematic inquiries into the origin of certain emotional disturbances of infants show that consistent maternal care is vital for the child's normal physical, psychic, and social development, indeed, for his survival. The most elementary precondition for consistent maternal care is the physical presence of the mother or her substitute.

It has, however, become increasingly apparent that children's development can also be stunted, and that they suffer damage of varying extent by the attention of, and close contact with, a mother

772

who dispenses what seems to be the "wrong kind of mothering."

A model of the mother-baby interaction is proposed to explicate the dynamics of the "wrong kind of mothering" and its consequences: my proposition is that the mutual exchanges between mother and baby consist in a give and take of action and reaction between the two partners, which requires from each of them both active and passive responses. These responses form series and chains, the single links of which consist in what I call "action cycles, " each completed in itself and at the same time anticipating the next link. I designated these seriated response exchanges as the "precursor of dialogue, " as a primal dialogue.

The dialogue acts as a vector of the baby's development, influencing its direction and stimulating it to adaptive efforts and psychic growth. It follows that inappropriate mothering (quantitatively as well as qualitatively) results in what is referred to at this time as the "derailment" of the primal dialogue.

Controlled experiments with animals, findings of experimental psychology, and, lastly, clinical findings illustrate the mechanics of the derailed dialogue and its sequelae.

In the cases under review a surfeit of stimulation, a psychic overloading, resulted in the derailment of dialogue. Overloading prevents its subject from completing actions or responses initiated by him. Long-lasting overload results in the cumulation of "incompleted action cycles." The sequelae of this cumulation are profound changes in the behavior of the individual. These changes are manifested in a departure from the norms of individual and social behavior patterns that are maladaptive for the individual and asyntonic with his society, that is, asocial.

The derailment of the dialogue is triggered, perhaps even caused, by the nature of the social setting. One setting, overpopulation, is extensively discussed in connection with an animal experiment, and the implications for the human community are examined.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Study on the Nature of Inspiration—Part I. Some Special Considerations Regarding the Phallic Phase

Phyllis Greenacre M.D.

Phyllis Greenacre

Phyllis Greenacre, M.D.
35 East 85 Street
New York 28, N.Y.

Phyllis Greenacre  

SUMMARY

In the foregoing account I have attempted to show how the stage is naturally set in the phallic phase for experiences of exciting, expanding, revelatory significance. Genital sensations and those arising from increased motor capacity give the foundation. The upright body position, combined with the ability to move up and down in space (to climb and to jump) give an increase in the sense of autonomy and separateness, and contribute to a greater ability to look at the body self and its functions with a modest critique. But further, the sense of lightness and invigoration of the period, as though the whole body were affected by deep breathing, comes coincidentally with the opportunity (or obligation) for a greater awareness of external space and time, and the confrontation with intangibles. In the natural symbolization of experience, these are intimately associated, then, with the air, the unseen conveyor of "contact" with the outer world without touch. In the awareness of the own functions, the consciousness of thinking, remembering, dreaming appears. But most of all, the experiences of this special period are influenced by the natural position of the phallic phase between the relatively simple early body-control conscience of the anal phase, and the subtle but elaborated and intense conscience extension of the oedipal phase. This results, as it must, in the further development of ideals and social regulations, but with some encroachment on the fresh expanding innocence of the infantile inspiration. It is my belief that the experiences of inspiration of the phallic period form the prototype for experiences of inspiration, whether these occur in the creative process of gifted individuals, or in a somewhat different form in religious experiences.

One may recall Melville's "What shall be grand in thee must needs be plucked at from the skies and dived for in the deep, and

29

featured in the unbodied air."13 But one must remember further that thought is never really unbodied. One way or another thought bears the imprint of the accompanying body. (More will be said of this in the next chapter.) Questions arise at once about the relation of the phallic inspiration of the child to the immediate developments of his life. Especially there is the question: "Why does the capacity for inspiration ordinarily then go into partial eclipse? Why does it lead to increasingly splendid wonder in the gifted child?" The intensity of the experience of the gifted child is a partial answer, and may indicate a basic biological variance. Vivid memories from the phallic period reveal how much the body sensations are reflected and expressed in the projections of the imagination about cosmic events. At this stage the infant poet, scientist, or philosopher is already budding. Then the oedipal period, following and overlapping the phallic one, may be more binding because more personal in the majority of children; while in the potential artist there is already recourse to the cosmic forms which are counterparts or alternates to the own parents (11).

Certainly it appears that the superego of the artist has a texture different from that of others to whom the artist, in his less personally oriented allegiances, may seem frighteningly irresponsible. In these terms it becomes much more understandable why the artist—and especially those in some of the scientific fields—may be on some kind of terms with a God who represents to them the mysterious drive of the creative force within themselves. This is somewhat different from the God who is so closely related to the personal father as is commonly true in the formalized group religious practices. But more will be said of this in subsequent chapters.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Sexual Enthrallment

Alan Parkin M.D.

Alan Parkin

Alan Parkin, M.D.
276 St. Clair Avenue W.
Toronto 7, Canada

Alan Parkin  

SUMMARY

In sexual enthrallment, a woman is held in a state of spellbound subservience by the phallic qualities of the man to whom she is attached. All her own individuality and initiative may be turned over to the man through whom she lives her life vicariously. However,

353

this state is characterized by an ambivalence which is expressed in a struggle, more or less overtly lived out, against the thralldom, and which in fact may overshadow the subservience to the man.

The fascination with and awe for the idealized object, and the longing for union with it, derive from the inevitable but intolerable differentiation of the infant-breast amalgam and the resulting loss of the infantile state of narcissistic omnipotence. The longing for union, or rather reunion, aims at re-establishing this state, and is accomplished by the incorporation of the now separately perceived and highly idealized breast and the subsequent merging of the embryonic ego with this ideal introject.

When the narcissistic self-esteem of the little girl is threatened again by her discovery of her lack of penis, it is sustained once more in a similar manner. The idealized penis is incorporated and assimilated to the ego ideal, and the ego fuses with it. In the resulting (primary) identification with the penis, infantile megalomania is regained in the fantasy of being the manifestation of the paternal omnipotence. She is thus driven between the Scylla of renouncing her autonomy as the captivated appendage to a larger whole and the Charybdis of being eaten in turn as the repository of phallic power and omnipotence she has become.

The state of sexual enthrallment may exist as a "pure" clinical picture and as a definite character type, or it may form the character background out of which may emerge diverse symptoms. Some phobics, for example, characterized by the fear of being alone, may be able to perform certain functions only if they are in the company of some person to whom they are in a relationship of sexual enthrallment. Fantasies of being a penis, the penis of the one to whom they are (emotionally) attached, may lead to fears of being separated as regressive forms of castration anxiety.

Although the state of sexual enthrallment is found typically in women, in whom in a milder form it may constitute an altogether unremarkable and ordinary relationship, it may also be found in men in whom a strong feminine identification is present (30), notably in passive male homosexuality (10) and in fetishism (35).

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Depersonalization

Walter Stewart M.D.

Walter Stewart

Walter Stewart  

Victor H. Rosen commented on the unusual nature of this panel: while no papers were to be presented, the members were far from unprepared; each had written on the subject extensively and had circulated his notes among the other members. In addition, the members had met on two occasions to share their views, a procedure which it was hoped would provide some order to the discussion and yet allow a degree of freedom and spontaneity. Rosen observed that depersonalization probably occurs more commonly than we realize and is often overlooked. James B. Cattell has described it as the third most frequent symptom seen in patients at a mental hospital, exceeded in frequency only by anxiety and depression. He suggested that when the analytic situation is confused, it may turn out to be evidence of the patient's unverbalized feelings of depersonalization or derealization. Rosen outlined briefly some questions which he thought the panel might attempt to discuss. For example: What is the essential phenomenology of the depersonalization-derealization syndrome? A definition of the boundaries of the subject is essential for all later discussion. What is the incidence of this phenomenon in mental illness? Does it carry a diagnostic or prognostic value? What experiences precipitate episodes of depersonalization? Are there specific external and internal conditions required for its appearance? How do the acute attacks of depersonalization come to an end? Are there specific libidinal points of fixation or defects in ego functioning? The last question would suggest some discussion of the role of perception and cognition and states of consciousness in the determination of depersonalization. The panel would undoubtedly discuss depersonalization as a defense. Rosen asked if one of the reasons for the use of depersonalization as a defense, might be the sudden shocklike nature of the trauma underlying it. How is depersonalization related to problems of identity?

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Consideration of Aging
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Consideration of Aging

Norman Zinberg M.D.

Norman Zinberg

Norman Zinberg  

In his introductory remarks, Martin Berezin called attention to the fact that this panel was held at the same time as that on child analysis, indicating the ever-widening scope of the use of psychoanalytic theory. The study of aging until now has been left to fields other than psychoanalysis, but the increased index of psychoanalytic interest is exemplified by the existence of the Boston Society for Gerontologic Psychiatry with a membership predominantly of analysts. Berezin discussed the difficulty in defining when "old age" begins, and the problems created by physical disability and cultural, youth-oriented attitudes toward the aged. He mentioned the longitudinal study of the superego, ego ideal, and the concept of ego strength as especially apt subjects open to analysts interested in psychoanalytic theory and willing to work with aged patients. The gradually increasing awareness that the aged are more interested in such things as the future or genital primacy than has previously been realized should facilitate psychoanalytic studies. Berezin stressed the need for many clinical observations, as from this panel, so that we do not generalize about the aged too quickly, and that, although we do not think in terms of actual psychoanalysis of the aged, we must bring to bear on the problem our entire experience as analysts and as human beings.

Douglas D. Bond presented "An Analyst's Observation on the Treatment of Older People." His purpose was to interest analysts in working with older persons. Although recognizing the physical deterioration connected with aging, and cautioning against Pollyanna-ish optimism about these later years, he suggested that analysts have been too concerned with certain misconceptions about aging. Particularly have they been overconcerned about psychological rigidity in the aged person. Bond stressed the analyst's wish to treat younger patients so that the years of work, effort, and money that go into analysis would yield a commensurate return.

[[RunningHead]]

The Emergence of Cognitive Psychology

Robert Holt Ph.D.

Robert Holt

Robert R. Holt, Ph.D.
Research Center for Mental Health New York University
21 Washington Place
New York, N.Y. 10003

Robert Holt  

One of the great unmistakable trends in psychology during the past decade and a half has been the emergence of a new and vigorous interest in cognition. This last term itself has experienced a revival of currency and respectability; once a scholastic term for knowledge, encountered in the classical threefold division of human function into cognition, affection, and conation, it has now shaken off such dusty and facultative connotations and is used boldly by neo-behaviorists who still feel a trifle shy about admitting an interest in thinking as a subject matter for psychology. The scope of the term is, to be sure, a good deal broader than thought processes: it comprises perceiving, judging, forming concepts, learning (especially that of a meaningful, verbal kind), imagining, fantasying, imaging, creating, and solving problems. One might try to summarize all this by saying that cognition deals with all aspects of symbolic behavior, in the broad sense, if it were not for the fact that the study of language is traditionally separated off into linguistics. Such boundaries are, of course, artificial, but the attempt to draw them helps us to recognize that cognitive psychology is growing actively at its peripheries as well as in its core area of thinking.

J. S. Bruner, E. Brunswik, L. Festinger, F. Heider, K. F. Muenzinger, C. E. Osgood, and D. Rapaport: CONTEMPORARY APPROACHES TO COGNITION. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 1957.

T. R. Sarbin, R. Taft, and D. E. Bailey: CLINICAL INFERENCE AND COGNITIVE THEORY. New York: Holt, Rinehart & Winston, 1960.

J. S. Bruner, J. Goodnow, and G. A. Austin: A STUDY OF THINKING. New York: Wiley, 1956.

E. S. Tauber and M. R. Green: PRELOGICAL EXPERIENCE. New York: Basic Books, 1959.

P. McKellar: IMAGINATION AND THINKING. New York: Basic Books, 1957.

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical and Theoretical Aspects of Religious Belief

James Mann M.D.

James Mann

James Mann  

Elvin V. Semrad pointed to the value of psychoanalysis in studying the affective importance of patients' belief systems. The questions of the objectivity of religious belief and religious values and the use of symbolic religious rites beyond ritualistic or magical attitudes are complicated and warrant our study and attention.

In "Incidental Clinical Psychoanalytic Research into Religious Belief, " Herman M. Serota stated that opportunities for the psychoanalyst to observe aspects of religious belief occur in almost every analysis. Analyst and analysand are immersed in the Judaeo-Christian milieu of our day, and pragmatic contacts with religious concepts attend all rites of passage from birth through death and mourning. The extensive inventories concerned with identity problems, especially with regard to those of one's children, and resulting conflicts, compromise formations, resolutions, rationalized adaptations, suppressions and repressions are likewise familiar processes in the study of psychic operations surrounding the questions of religious belief.

Moral control of both sexual and aggressive acts is usually placed in religious frames of reference vaguely conceived but nonetheless stringent. It emerges in the form of superego themes or their derivatives. The superego introjects as revealed in the transference often come to bear the behavioral attributes of the church whose doctrines they once espoused. Religion conceived in these terms must necessarily be object related but is correspondingly analyzable.

As Freud pointed out, it is still customary today to attribute religious ideas to divine revelation and to object to disbelief. He then defines illusion as a belief in which wish fulfillment as a motivation is a prominent factor and the relation to reality is disregarded.

[[RunningHead]]

On Play and the Psychopathology of Golf

Carl Adatto M.D.

Carl Adatto

Carl Adatto, M.D.
6205 Garfield Street
New Orleans 18, La.

Carl Adatto  

SUMMARY

This paper attempts to document through analytic material from patients that the game of golf has a wide range of intrapsychic

840

meanings to the patient. Because of maturation, the adult's play differs manifestly from that of the child, but like the dream the latent unconscious meaning is similarly active and should be used as a basis for comparison. Analysis demonstrated that while a game may have certain common symbolic meanings, golf has no single meaning, even for the same individual. It is proposed that the theories of the function of play, especially those emphasizing mastery of the environment, mastery of painful experience, and pleasure in function, should be uniformly extended to include play activity throughout the life of the individual, and not be limited to childhood or special categories of adults.

[[RunningHead]]

Theory of Psychoanalytic Therapy

Leon Altman M.D.

Leon Altman

Leon Altman  

Nathaniel Ross stated that while no basic contributions to the theory of psychoanalytic therapy have been made within recent years, concentration on individual problems has brought refinement to our understanding of the hypothetical foundations of the analytic situation and procedure. This is particularly true with reference to the development and expansion of the structural theory. While the chief area of concentration has been upon ego psychology, this does not exclude the need for continued study of the roles of id and superego.

The panel's purpose was to put into comprehensive form the larger ideas which now constitute our theory of therapy and to take an over-all view which has not been done since the panel discussion of the International in 1936. The chief aim of our panel, Ross said, was to establish a perspective and to point to areas for further investigation. He emphasized the need for a balanced point of view in order to avoid misleading unitary, exclusive, "cultist" approaches. We must examine all the forces which enter into the analytic situation and understand its therapeutic operations from the dynamic, genetic, economic, structural, and adaptive aspects.

The intention, then, was to cover a broad range of topics but, as will be seen, a focus developed and gathered momentum. After a discussion of general theoretical factors operative in psychoanalytic therapy, Ross hoped to follow the theory of the operation of specific factors, divided into three parts: (1) from the standpoint of the patient; (2) from the standpoint of the analyst; and (3) what may be learned about the theory of therapy from its failures.

In presenting his admittedly very brief survey of the literature, Ross first cited Freud's conceptions of the aims of analytic therapy: (1) making the unconscious conscious; (2) replacing the id with the ego; and (3) freeing the ego for future development. These remain an ample framework for

300 Central Park West
New York 24, N.Y.

K. Eissler  

I am already looking forward to seeing [my biographers] go astray.—FREUD, 1885

When Freud wrote these lines to his bride, he would surely have been surprised if anyone had told him that the study of his future papers would some day make the biographer's task of interpreting that very letter less hazardous than it was at the time when he was expressing a somewhat malicious joy over the future biographer's anticipated failure. Likewise, he probably would have been quite incredulous if he had been told then that the publication of 315 of his letters would one day arouse the greatest interest. For so it does, not only among those who are interested in psychoanalysis, but also among those concerned with the cultural process, or, more specifically, with the creative process.

This selection of Freud's letters, excellently put together by Ernst Freud, offers a panorama of rewarding richness. The reader can obtain a glimpse of Freud's adolescence and a deep view of his courtship with Martha Bernays; can watch him in his dealings with his wife, mother, children, friends, pupils, or on his travels to Paris, Dresden or Rome. One listens to the words of consolation that Freud wrote when friends fell sick or were bereaved of those whom they loved; one learns of Freud's emotional response to the death of his mother and his daughter, and to his own unimaginable sufferings when he was seized by a terrible disease.

LETTERS OF SIGMUND FREUD. Selected and edited by Ernst L. Freud. Translated by Tania and James Stern. New York: Basic Books, 1960, viii + 470 pp. $6.75; London: Hogarth Press, 1961, 464 pp.; Sigmund Freud: Briefe 1873-1939. Frankfurt: S. Fischer Verlag, 1960, 511 pp.

IN HIS CLASSIC paper "The Image of the Artist: A Psychological Study of the Role of Tradition in Ancient Biographies, " Ernst Kris (15) called attention to a specific pattern of the family romance, which over many different epochs in the history of art, including the present day, has reappeared as authentic biography of the artist. According to this formula, the future artist is represented as an obscure poor child, often a shepherd boy, whose creative gifts are discovered accidently by an established master, who subsequently becomes the child's sponsor and protector. At a Memorial Meeting for Ernst Kris in 1957 Greenacre (11) presented a paper demonstrating the application of these observations on the family romance to five celebrated men, one of whom, the explorer and nearly perennial rescuer, Henry Morton Stanley, apparently played an important if unwitting role in a family romance fantasy of Joseph Conrad, the subject of this presentation.

Roy Schafer Ph.D.

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer, Ph.D.
Yale University Dept. of Health
Box 1505-A Yale Station
New Haven, Conn.

Roy Schafer  

SUMMARY

I have discussed the analytic investigation of the occasions, meanings, functions, and changes of emotion. I have surveyed eight general aspects and reference points of this investigation: affect

297

existence, affect formation, affect strength (including optimal strength), affect stimuli (internal and external), affect complexity and paradox, affect location (in terms of time, layer, person, and zone, and with particular emphasis on so-called borrowed affects and repersonification of affects), affect communication (interpersonal and intrapsychic, with particular reference to empathy), and affect history. It is obvious that the analysis of affects is concerned not only with feelings but with the ideas, attitudes, fantasies, impulses, defenses, and all the other familiar major categories of analytic data from which they are inseparable. Affect analysis is therefore only one facet of the total psychoanalytic process.

Narcissism and the Prepuberty Ego Ideal

Ives Hendrick M.D.

Ives Hendrick

Ives Hendrick, M.D.
84 Mount Vernon Street
Boston 8, Mass.

Ives Hendrick  

IN HIS classic paper, "On Narcissism" (5), Sigmund Freud proposed a new theory, that the object of libido is originally the self, or partial representations of the self, that this is the original libidinal investment in infancy, and that recognition of this has important relevance to the understanding of schizophrenia, hypochondria, and other important mental problems. At this time Freud went so far as to distinguish primary narcissism, the original state of "autoerotic" sensory pleasure without object representations, from secondary narcissism, the libidinal cathexis of later mental representations of the body and the self. This was a beginning, a very important beginning, a hint from the master, which Freud did not himself pursue a great deal further, nor did he fully reveal how important the understanding of the details of the development of narcissism would eventually become. During the intervening fifty years, since Freud's classic paper in 1914, it has become self-evident that a theory of narcissism is an indispensable intellectual tool for the dynamic and economic psychoanalysis of many problems.

Yet only in quite recent years have several extensive studies based on details of clinical experience supplied a long-needed example of the complex and varied phenomena which the theory of narcissism can clarify (14), (15).

These comments are based on the discussion of a paper "Narcissism and the Ego Ideal, " by John M. Murray, Presented before the Boston Psychoanalytic Society, March 27, 1963.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Letters to Ernst Simmel

Frances Deri and David Brunswick Ph.D.

David Brunswick

David Brunswick  

Introduction

DAVID BRUNSWICK, Ph.D. AND RUTH LACHENBRUCH

AMONG THE followers of Freud, of the early as well as of the later years, none was more loyal, more devoted, more meticulous in adhering to basic principles in psychoanalysis than Ernst Simmel. Out of his experience as a medical staff officer in the German Army in World War I he wrote his well-known paper on "War Neurosis and Psychic Trauma, " which evoked Freud's first letter to him.

In this letter Freud foreshadows his expectation that Simmel will make further contributions to the problems of mental disease. Beginning with 1918 there was an interchange of letters between the two men that continued until 1939. Although there is in the collection1 only one letter from Simmel (the one that summarized the culmination of his efforts to establish a psychoanalytic sanitarium, Schloss Tegel, 1927), there is evidence in Freud's letters that Simmel always looked to Freud as the leader and guide who would cut through the knotty problems that constantly arose in the developing science.

Published by permission of Sigmund Freud Copyrights, Ltd.

Submitted October 10, 1962

Grace Abbate M.D.

Grace Abbate

Grace Abbate  

Elisabeth R. Geleerd pointed to the importance of presenting clinical material from the analyses of children in different developmental phases, with examination of the variations in technique necessitated by such age differences. In a paper entitled "Introduction: A Critique of Recent Developments in Child Analysis, " Eleanor Galenson attempted to establish a correlation between the refinements of child analysis since Anna Freud's 1928 publication, The Psychoanalytic Treatment of Children, and the advances in general psychoanalytic knowledge and child development since then. In her formulation of the child analytic situation and process, as first conceived and practiced, Galenson emphasized the view that the child serves partly as a transference object and also as a real new object with whom the child can establish a new relationship. Therefore, the child analyst could never be the "shadowy" figure to the same extent as is the adult analyst. Besides, the psychological immaturity of the child demands that the analyst assume an additional role—that of an auxiliary ego and superego.

She spoke about the gradual and subtle changes in the areas of preoedipal events, defense mechanisms, and lines of development, referring to the work of Anna Freud, Spitz, and Mahler, and the theoretical papers of Hartmann, Kris, and Loewenstein in demonstrating the central importance of the mother in the instinctual and ego development of the child.

[[RunningHead]]

Some Aspects of Psychoanalytic Methodology

Bernard Fine M.D.

Bernard Fine

Bernard Fine  

The panel, as arranged by the Chairman, was designed to facilitate a maximum of open participation and discussion. Following the opening presentation, this was accomplished very effectively by interspersing the other papers throughout the discussion at the discretion of the Chairman. There was a brief introduction by Charles Brenner outlining the above method of procedure and pointing out the confluence of the subject matter in each paper, essentially revolving around the crucial role of the psychoanalytic situation in methodology and theory formation.

In "Notes on Psychoanalytic Methodology: Empathy, the Analyst, and the Analytic Situation, " Bernard Fine pointed to the necessity for asking the "good and proper question" in any scientific endeavor, and emphasized the possibility of being misled into procedures which are not applicable to psychoanalysis, or into inappropriate types of quantification or experimental approaches, if one does not understand the basic concepts and problems involved. Fine focused on an interesting and unexpected parallel between psychoanalysis and microphysics (elementary particle physics) in that both are growing research disciplines and are at the "frontier" of scientific knowledge. In contrast to the "finished" physical systems, such as optics and planetary mechanics, microphysics will remain an unsettled dynamic research science because of its conceptual framework. This will be equally true of psychoanalysis, particularly because of the nature of its basic instrument of observation, the empathic and intuitive processes of the analyst. In both sciences, inquiry is directed not to the rearrangement of old facts and explanations leading to more formal patterns but rather to the discovery of new patterns of explanation. Physicists and psychoanalysts alike rarely find laws or establish theoretical concepts by enumerating and summarizing observables.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Parameters in Child Analysis

Samuel Weiss M.D.

Samuel Weiss

Samuel Weiss, M.D.
737 N. Michigan Ave.
Chicago 11, Ill.

Samuel Weiss  

SUMMARY

This paper concerns itself with the technique of child analysis and the theory of such technique. It raises questions about the validity of many by-now traditional procedures, such as the use of candy, birthday presents, consultations with parents, wooing the child, etc. These procedures constitute deviations from a more classical technique such as exists in adult analysis.

A basic model technique is needed so that one can institute deviations when they are deemed essential and so that their course through the analysis can be followed and ultimately be analyzed. It is necessary in the course of a successful analysis that these deviations, these parameters, be analyzed.

Illustrations of parameters are given, showing how they complicate the therapeutic process and aid or interfere with it. Some parameters were successfully analyzed and some were not; their effect is noted.

Adult analysis has attempted to formulate a theory of technique. A major question is whether this theory has a universal

598

applicability—to the phases of childhood as well as to adult life. Does child analysis have conditions that differ from adult analysis in a fundamental way? Do developmental considerations, such as the immaturity of the child's ego, alter this theory in a fundamental way? Have our preconceptions about the structure of personality so directed our theories of technique that we are unable to validate anything other than what we already believe?

Haug (6) poses the question whether technique should be derived from a theory of technique or whether the theory should be derived from what we find works and doesn't work. Her answer: "Not only is each needed, but both are. It is the going back and forth from the one method of approach to the other which alone makes it possible to arrive at a technique and at a theory which are both valid and progressive."

This paper raises more questions than it answers. It is not definitive, it is not an end point. Hopefully, it is a beginning.

On the Identity Crisis in American Psychoanalysis

Maxwell Gitelson M.D.

Maxwell Gitelson

Maxwell Gitelson, M.D.
55 East Washington Street

Maxwell Gitelson  

IT IS THIRTY YEARS since I have been committed to psychoanalysis. These years span nearly its entire modern period, most of which has been centered in the United States. The events which changed the world after Hitler came to power halted the development of psychoanalysis in Europe and moved it to the western hemisphere with a surviving foothold only in Britain. By far the larger number of European psychoanalysts found a haven and an opportunity for many scientifically productive years in this country. But these years have also seen the gradual appearance of social issues and problems like those which confronted Freud and his early collaborators. I have been in close touch with and to a considerable extent involved in these revived issues and problems and I propose to review and discuss them.

I

Speaking before the A.A.A.S. at its Atlanta meeting in 1955, Harold K. Schilling (21) referred to science as having the qualities of a "social enterprise" which involved interaction, cooperation, and sharing. He pointed out that "intersubjective testability" (Sullivan's "consensual validation") is a synonym for "objectivity" and that the word "empirical" refers primarily to social rather than solitary experience. "Science, " he said, "is [of necessity] communal.

Presented under the title, "Psychoanalytic Introspections, Retrospects and Prospects, " at the Sunday Morning Plenary Session of the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, May 3, 1964, Los Angeles.

Submitted May 11, 1964

"Studies on Hysteria"—A Methodological Evaluation

John Gedo M.D. , Melvin Sabshin M.D. , Leo Sadow M.D. and Nathan Schlessinger M.D.

Nathan Schlessinger

John E. Gedo, M.D.
Melvin Sabshin, M.D.
Leo Sadow, M.D.
Nathan Schlessinger, M.D.
912 South Wood Street
Chicago 12, Ill.

Nathan Schlessinger  

SUMMARY

A historical review of the clinical evidence from which the basic concepts of psychoanalysis were derived must begin with Studies on Hysteria. A schema developed by Waelder for classifying the propositions of a clinical science according to their degree of remoteness from the data of observation has been utilized in reducing Studies on Hysteria into a series of discrete statements. All these statements have been cross-correlated, yielding a complex chart demonstrating which propositions at higher levels of abstraction could have been derived by induction from the clinical evidence presented and from the intermediate interpretations and generalizations based upon it.

This method has been found useful in studying the scientific styles of the authors of the Studies and may contribute to the understanding of scientific creativity in our field. We have found that in this work the styles of the two authors are divergent and complementary. Breuer displayed great freedom in hypothesis building based on deductive reasoning, while Freud subjected his inspirational hunches to thorough inductive revision in the light of the clinical evidence available to him.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Uses of the Terms "Autoerotism" and "Narcissism"

Mark Kanzer M.D.

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer, M.D.
80 Park Ave.
New York 16, N.Y.

Mark Kanzer  

SUMMARY

Autoerotism and narcissism, terms which fundamentally have the same meaning, self-love, and were derived from the same source, the work of Havelock Ellis, were introduced into Freud's writings some ten years apart. The first term, used in conjunction with the developing libido theory, has always referred to sensual pleasure derived from one's own body; the second term, used in conjunction with the development of the ego concept, has usually implied an attitude of love toward the self which involves a more advanced psychic organization in which the sexual aims have been delibidinized and at least a rudimentary self image has been formed. Consequently, autoerotism and narcissism have been differently defined with relation to each other depending upon evolving psychoanalytic views with respect to these basic phenomena.

The particular definition which has gained most currency—that the autoerotic instincts must be prior to a state of narcissism which depends upon an established unity of the ego—was quickly abandoned by Freud himself when he came to see narcissism not as an advanced autoerotic organization of the libido but as an ego organization that functions from the beginning of mental life. Autoerotism, in this framework, reverted to its former status as a mode of pleasure to be obtained from one's own body, more specifically the erogenous zones, at any time in life. Drives may be primarily or secondarily autoerotic; the description of drives as pleasure-seeking or object-seeking is in itself a residue of the early stage of depth psychology and of the empirical references to objects that prevailed prior to the metapsychological distinctions that had to be made between real objects (which are not directly accessible to the drives) and psychic presentations (which may be).

The definitions of autoerotism and narcissism were further clarified with the delineation of the self concept. The suggestion is made that a structurally satisfying comparison of autoerotism and narcissism would emphasize in the one the pleasurable experiences

537

for the ego to be derived from somatic sensations and associated with the direct discharge of drive energy and in the other the pleasurable feelings of identity between the self image and the ego ideal associated with the discharge of neutral energy. Primary narcissism could then refer either to ego cathexis or self cathexis (15) depending on whether reference is made to the ego system or to the self image.

Autoerotism could be more specifically defined if described with respect to the particular source of pleasure (oral, phallic, motor, etc.). The question would also have to be raised whether it should not be confined to self-induced activities, for it is difficult to distinguish even in the infant a pleasurable experience from one's own body that is not associated in fact or in imagination with the presence of another person, or to conceive of alloerotic pleasures that are not also autoerotic. Perhaps the only real use for the term is that which has prevailed from the beginning: a pragmatic description of pleasure to be obtained from one's own body in the absence of another person.

Book Notices

THE QUEST FOR THE FATHER. A STUDY OF THE DARWIN-BUTLER CONTROVERSY, AS A CONTRIBUTION TO THE UNDERSTANDING OF THE CREATIVE INDIVIDUAL. The Freud Anniversary Lectures, The New York Psychoanalytic Institute. By Phyllis Greenacre. New York: International Universities Press, 1963, 128 pp., $3.00.

An introduction presents a general discussion of the rationale and methodology of the application of psychoanalysis to the study of creative individuals, referring to the stimulating as well as the obstructive effect neurosis may have upon them. The specific study here taken up centers about the controversy between Samuel Butler, who first embraced and then violently attacked Darwin's theories, and the scientist. Similarities and likenesses between the personalities of these outstanding men, in terms of their backgrounds and personalities, are elaborated around the very strong ambivalence in their relationships to their fathers and grandfathers, as furnishing the central clue to an understanding of the struggle between them into which, it is clear, they possessed no insight. Butler's desperate and unavailing search for an omnipotent, loving father, unsatisfactorily acted out in adopting mediocre protégés who could never realize his desires for him, was at first channeled into an extravagant espousal of Darwin, to be followed after a number of years by bitter, rancorous attacks upon the latter, who, bewildered by their fury, did not reply to them. The author believes that "the eternal search for the father" is "an integral part of [the] oedipal problems" of many artists.

THE ASTHMATIC CHILD. PSYCHOSOMATIC APPROACH TO PROBLEMS AND TREATMENT. Edited by Henry I. Schneer. New York, Evanston & London: Hoeber Medical Division, Harper & Row, 1963, xii + 260 pp., $6.50.

Represented are somatic, psychiatric, psychological, and psychoanalytic studies of the asthmatic child. Chapters comprise such subject matter as diagnosis of asthma, psychodynamics, and personality of the asthmatic child, mother-child relationship, family constellations, analytic studies, institutional treatment, etc. Among the authors are Murray Peshkin, Harold Abramson, Peter Knapp, Lucie Jessner, Henry Schneer.
